coredata.dc:title: A comparison of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots supported on the surface of hollow and solid carbon spheres, and solid silica spheres
coredata.dc:creator: Mkhari, Orlette
Ntuli, Themba D.
Coville, Neil J.
Nxumalo, Edward N.
Maubane-Nkadimeng, Manoko S.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (Cdots) have attracted many researchers due to their strong optical photoluminescence (PL) properties; however, due to their small size they agglomerate in the solid state which makes it difficult to remove them from solution. To overcome these problems, Cdots can be deposited on a support, but to date, few systematic studies of Cdots-support systems have been reported, especially those on carbon supports. Herein, we report a systematic study of the effect of the use of N-Cdots (5, 10, 15 and 20%) supported on the surface of hollow carbon spheres (HCSs), solid carbon spheres (SCSs) and silica spheres (SSs). The morphology of the Cdots and their composites (Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs) was studied by TEM, TGA, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis and PL techniques. The FTIR, XRD and TGA data suggest different interactions of the Cdots with the supports. The PL quantum yield for bare Cdots was 34% and this value was dependent on the supports, in the order Cdots/SSs > Cdots/HCSs > Cdots/SCSs due to different interactions between the Cdots and the support.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (Cdots) have attracted many researchers due to their strong optical photoluminescence (PL) properties; however, due to their small size they agglomerate in the solid state which makes it difficult to remove them from solution. To overcome these problems, Cdots can be deposited on a support, but to date, few systematic studies of Cdots-support systems have been reported, especially those on carbon supports. Herein, we report a systematic study of the effect of the use of N-Cdots (5, 10, 15 and 20%) supported on the surface of hollow carbon spheres (HCSs), solid carbon spheres (SCSs) and silica spheres (SSs). The morphology of the Cdots and their composites (Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs) was studied by TEM, TGA, XRD, FTIR, UV–vis and PL techniques. The FTIR, XRD and TGA data suggest different interactions of the Cdots with the supports. The PL quantum yield for bare Cdots was 34% and this value was dependent on the supports, in the order Cdots/SSs > Cdots/HCSs > Cdots/SCSs due to different interactions between the Cdots and the support.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is the sixth most common element in the universe and one of the essential chemical elements required by living organisms [1]. It serves as a building block for all organic compounds and it plays a major role in nanoscience, materials science and engineering [1,2]. To date, numerous carbon allotropes (for example, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphite, amorphous carbon and diamond) have been made and their nanostructures studied in detail [3,4]. This relates to the properties associated with carbon such as light absorption across the UV–visible region, variable surface area and flexibility to form a range of different morphologies [5].

Nanostructured carbons continue to reveal new allotropes and one of the most recently discovered is the carbon dot (Cdot), with size ≤ 10 nm [6,7]. Cdots have interesting photoluminescence properties, diverse intrinsic electronic and magnetic properties, good electron transfer rates and biocompatibility, all of which make them attractive for use in many applications such as optoelectronic devices, biomedical imaging and catalysis [8,9]. Cdots have a spherical or near spherical shape and are made by the layering of sp2 graphene domains next to and on top of each other [10]. The Cdots surface can be passivated with O and H atoms and also importantly, sp3 or sp2 carbon atoms containing various functional groups [11,12]. Due to their limited size, their large number of structural defects and their abundant surface functional groups (containing typically O and N atoms) [13], they possess an electronic bandgap and a high electron transfer in the visible light region [14]. Their wide range of light absorption and efficient conversion of light energy into chemical energy makes them good candidates for accelerating photochemical reactions [15].

Cdots have been prepared from many different carbon sources such as foodstuffs, biomass, proteins, small organic molecules, sugars and numerous waste materials [16,17]. Once made, there are difficulties with their use, due to their small size which leads to filtration issues when retrieving them from solution. After removal from solution they can form carbonaceous aggregates that can lead to aggregation-induced luminescence quenching whereby their photoluminescence (PL) emissions are affected during application [18,17]. The deteriorated photoluminescence spectrum can lead to poor quantum yields and an inadequate understanding of the origin of the photoluminescent properties of Cdots which can also result in poor spectral interpretations [19].

One method to address the drawbacks associated with Cdots listed above is to support them on the surface of insoluble solids [20–22]. Cdots that have been supported on various surfaces have often been shown to have improved properties relative to their unsupported counterparts [23,24]. Furthermore, the addition of the Cdots to a surface can introduce functional groups to the support through the functional groups on the Cdots [25]. The synthesis of Cdots supported on silica spheres has received more attention as compared to other supports. The attention arose due to the advantageous properties that the silica support can provide, such as its structural morphology, large surface area, higher loading capacity (especially hollow mesoporous silica) and the ability to avoid interaction of supported Cdots particles. In addition, Cdots supported on silica supports have been found to show an enhanced PL quantum yield [26,27]. In addition to silica supports, Cdots supported on the surface of N-doped TiO2 nanorods have shown improved electrochemical performance when used as an anode material in sodium-ion batteries [28]. Thus, it is clear that supported Cdots show promise as a new type of reagent for use in various chemical processes such as adsorption, energy storage and catalysis. Table S1 shows some of the supports that have been used to make Cdots/support materials.

Interestingly, although there are numerous examples of Cdots/carbon materials that have been reported, very few systematic studies have been performed using carbon as a support for the Cdots. Examples of the use of Cdots carbon materials include the use of multi-wall carbon nanotubes as supports [29]. Deng et al., reported the synthesis of magnetic carbon nanotubes modified with Cdots and how the Cdots improved the removal of carbamazepine from wastewater [30]. Li et al., made Cdots supported on cytopore and demonstrated the rapid adsorption of Cd using the Cdots/cytopore material [29]. Cdots have also been placed inside hollow carbon spheres [31–33]. In addition, Chen et al., have made polyvynil alcohol/Cdots and showed how their fluorescence properties were affected by the presence of the Cdots content [34]. Other examples of Cdots/carbon materials are given in Table S1.

However, to our knowledge no systematic study has to date been undertaken to investigate the interaction of Cdots with carbon support surfaces. In this study, we have chosen to investigate the effect of hollow carbon spheres (HCSs) and solid carbon spheres (SCSs) as supports for the N-doped Cdots. HCSs have the ability to provide a thin carbon porous shell that provides a high surface area (and interesting porosity) and the ability to readily allow the detection of the Cdots by transmission electron microscopy [31]. We have also made solid silica spheres (SSs) that allows for a comparison of the effect of silica with carbon and also with the HCSs, on the properties of the supported Cdots. The composites have been studied by numerous techniques and the optical properties (PL emission) and the relative PL quantum yield (PLQY) of the Cdots/supports have been compared.

Chitosan (C6H11NO4)n with low molecular weight (50,000 Da) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (75–85% deacetylated) and deionized water (resistivity > 18.2 MU cm−1) was used as a solvent. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%, Sigma Aldrich), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 25%, Fluka) and ethanol (EtOH, 96%, Merck) were used for the synthesis of the SSs. The SSs were coated with resorcinol (99%, Sigma Aldrich), formaldehyde (≥34.5 wt%, Sigma Aldrich,) and hexadecyltrimethyl-ammonium bromide (CTAB; 98%, Aldrich). A 10% hydrofluoric acid (HF; 48%, Associated Chemical Enterprise) solution in water was used for the etching reactions.

The synthesis of the N-doped Cdots was carried out using a modified hydrothermal carbonization method adopted from a previous study [35]. Typically, chitosan (1 g) was dispersed in 50 mL deionized water and then sonicated for 30 min at a temperature of 40 °C. The sonicated mixture was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon cup that was placed inside an autoclave reactor. The synthesis was carried out at a temperature of 200 °C for 3 h after using a heating ramp of 2 °C/min, starting from room temperature (typically 25 °C) [36,11]. After the reaction time, the sample was allowed to cool to room temperature, and then 200 mL of distilled water was added to fully disperse the product containing the Cdots. The resulting solution was filtered and centrifuged at 18,000 rpm for 20 min to further remove any insoluble black particles. The supernatant (Cdots) solution was further filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane to remove all unwanted residuals. The product was dried using rotary evaporator at 60 °C, followed by drying at a temperature of 70 °C to obtain a solid Cdots product (~26% yield).

The SSs were synthesized from a mixture of ethanol, deionized H2O, ammonia (25%) and TEOS using a modification of literature procedures [37,38]. A solution of EtOH (390 mL), distilled water (50 mL) and NH4OH (20 mL) was stirred vigorously for 15 min, then TEOS (50 mL) was added to the solution and the mixture was stirred for a further 24 h. After 24 h the solution was centrifuged at 18,000 rpm for 15 min followed by drying of the white silica sphere powder in an oven at a temperature of 100 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the hollow carbon spheres (HCSs)
ce:section.ce:para: HCSs were synthesized by using the SSs prepared in Section 2.3 as a template [39]. The SSs (1 g) were added to a mixture of ethanol (100 mL), H2O (75 mL) and NH4OH (4 mL) followed by sonication for 30 min. A second mixture containing resorcinol (0.5 g), formaldehyde solution (4 mL) and CTAB (2 g) in ethanol (150 mL) were sonicated for 30 min. The two mixtures were combined and stirred at room temperature for 24 h to form a resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) coating around the SSs. The resulting composite (SSs@RF) was filtered and washed repeatedly with water followed by drying in an oven at 100 °C for 12 h. The SSs@RF was then carbonized inside a horizontal tube by heating under a nitrogen flow (50 mL/min) at 650 °C for 3 h. HCSs were formed by removing the SSs template by etching using HF (10%) overnight. This was followed by drying the HCSs at 100 °C oven overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the solid carbon spheres (SCSs)
ce:section.ce:para: Solid carbon spheres (SCSs) were synthesized using a modified method reported by Mutuma et al. [40] The synthesis of SCSs was done in a vertical CVD reactor fitted with a quartz tube (2.4 cm i.d. × 1.15 m length). Typically, Ar gas at 550 sccm from a gas delivery line was allowed to pass through the quartz tube and flow through the bubbler as the reactor was heated to 1000 °C at 10 °C/min. Once the temperature of the furnace reached 1000 °C, acetylene (C2H2) gas (25 sccm) from another delivery line was introduced to the reactor, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 2 h and the SCSs product was collected in a 3 L round bottom flask. After 2 h, the product in the round bottom flask was purified by a Soxhlet extraction method where 200 mL toluene was heated at a temperature of 120 °C for 24 h. The product was dried on a paper towel and the SCSs sample was placed in an oven (80 °C, 6 h) for further drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of Cdots onto the surface of SSs, HCSs and SCSs
ce:section.ce:para: The Cdots were loaded on the surface of the SSs, HCSs and SCSs using modified previous methods [20,41]. Typically, two solution mixtures were prepared in two different beakers. A desired amount of Cdots (5%, 10%, 15% and 20%) were dispersed in a mixture of 20 ml water and 10 mL of ethanol under sonication for 15 min. In a second separate beaker, 100 mg of the support (SSs, HCSs and SCSs) was added to a mixture of NH4OH (1 mL) and H2O (10 mL) and the mixture was sonicated for 15 min. The two mixtures containing the support and Cdots were combined, sonicated for 15 min, and then stirred overnight at room temperature. This was followed by drying of the product in a rotary evaporator at 60 °C and then in an oven at 100 °C for 6 h.

The size and shape of the Cdots, HCSs, SSs, SCSs and all composites were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a FEI Technai G2 Spirit electron microscope operated at 120 kV. ImageJ software was used to determine the particle size distribution of all samples (TEM images), for each sample more than 100 particles were measured. The above-mentioned materials were also studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Bruker D2 phaser equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å), an operating voltage of 30 kV and current of 10 mA. The thermal stability of the synthesized materials was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using a Perkin Elmer TA TGA Q500. About 10 mg of each sample was heated up to a temperature of 900 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under air. The elemental composition of the Cdots was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) obtained on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD XPS spectrometer equipped with both an Al (monochromatic) and dual Al/Mg anode and a charge neutralizer. The Cdots chemical composition was further complimented with CHNS analysis. The functional groups on the materials were recorded using Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy in the range 4000–500 cm−1 on a Bruker Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer. Finally, the optical properties of the materials were investigated using a Varian Cary Eclipse EL04103870 fluorescence spectrophotometer and a SPECORD 210 plus UV–vis spectrophotometer.

By definition, Cdots have a diameter ≤ 10 nm. Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of Cdots and Cdots/support composites. The corresponding particle size distribution of all the samples is given in Fig. S1. Fig. 1(a) shows the bare Cdots formed from chitosan having particle sizes ranging from 1 nm to 8.5 nm with the most dominant particles having sizes of 4.8 ± 0.94 nm. The particles were found to be quasi or nearly spherical in shape and were homogeneously distributed with no agglomeration [42]. Fig. 1(b) shows the SSs formed by using a modified Stober method which is based on the hydrolysis of silicon alkoxides that results in the formation of silica species with various shapes and sizes depending on the synthesis parameters used [43]. The SSs were spherically shaped with particle sizes ranging from 280 nm to 460 nm with an average size of 379 ± 38 nm. Fig. 1(c) shows the Cdots/SSs composite in which the Cdots have been incorporated on the SSs and are seen to be well dispersed on the surface of SSs with no agglomeration. The particle sizes of the Cdots on the surface of SSs ranged from 3 nm to 10 nm having average diameters of 7.2 ± 0.99 nm [24]. The HCSs were found to have sizes ranging from 300 nm to 440 nm with average particle size of 380 ± 22 nm (Fig. 1(d)). Fig. 1(e) shows the Cdots/HCSs composites i.e., Cdots incorporated on the surface of HCSs. The Cdots were well distributed with minor agglomeration on the surface of spherical HCSs. The sizes of the Cdots on the surface of HCSs ranged from 3 nm to 8 nm with an average diameter of 5.1 ± 0.94 nm which is comparable to the diameter of the bare Cdots.

In Fig. 1(f) and (g), the TEM images of SCSs and Cdots/SCSs composites are presented. The SCSs were observed to be spherically shaped with particle sizes ranging from 60 to 200 nm with average diameter of 111 ± 27 nm [40]. The shape and size of SCSs were maintained after incorporation of Cdots on the surface with Cdots maintaining their sizes below 10 nm and having average diameter of 3.79 ± 0.68 nm. The dispersion of the Cdots onto the support of the SSs, HCSs and SCSs can be attributed to the higher dispersion of Cdots in a mixture of ethanol and water. Addition of ammonia to the solution mixture also facilitated the incorporation and dispersion of Cdots into the surfaces of the support [20]. Furthermore, the dispersion of the Cdots particles onto the surface of the support is in accordance with Derjagin-Laundau-Verwey-Overbeek theory which proposed the instability of smaller particles in the presence of bigger particles, as such the smaller particles (Cdots) bind to the surface of support (SSs, HCSs and SCSs) and avoid coagulation or agglomeration with each other [44]. In our case, minor agglomeration of Cdots was observed on the surface of HCSs and they were well dispersed on SSs and SCSs. This is likely to be due to the lower concentration of Cdots particles on the surface of the supports [44].

Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to determine the functional groups present in the materials. Fig. 2 (a) shows the stretching and bending vibrations of Cdots, SSs and SSs incorporated Cdots composites (5–20% incorporation).

The as synthesized Cdots were observed to have a broad peak between 3000 and 3661 cm−1 (centred at 3305 cm−1) which corresponds to the overlap of N-H/O-H stretching vibrations, while the peaks at 2935/2880 cm−1 and 918/850 cm−1 are ascribed to the CH stretching and bending vibrations, respectively [42,45]. The COO−/C=O, CC, NH bending mode, CC and/or C-O-C vibrational modes are observed at 1651/1583 cm−1, 1408 cm−1, 1325 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1, respectively [35,45]. The chemical composition and bonding environments of the bare Cdots was also studied using XPS and CHNS (see Fig. S2 and Table S3 in the Supplementary).

The bare spheres have three peaks at 1069 cm−1 and 946/782 cm−1 which corresponds to Si-O-Si stretching and bending vibrations, respectively (Fig. 2(a)) [41,26]. After the incorporation of the Cdots on the surface of SSs new peaks associated with Cdots were observed. The intensities of the peaks due to the incorporation of the Cdots onto the surface of the SSs increased with an increase in the Cdots loading, i.e., 20%Cdots/SSs > 15%Cdots/SSs > 10%Cdots/SSs > 5%Cdots/SSs (Fig. 2(b)). A comparison of the FTIR spectra of the 20%Cdots/SSs with the Cdots (and SSs) indicated that the ratio of peaks at ca 1600 cm−1 and those at ca 1400 cm−1 (I1400/I1600) increased after addition of the Cdots to the SSs. Peaks that have been lost appear to be due to the COO− and NH2 absorptions. A small peak shift also occurred in the 1600 cm−1 suggesting some bonding interaction between the Cdots and the SSs support. The peak shift increased slightly as the Cdots loading was increased (5% = 1633 cm−1, 10% and 15% = 1638 cm−1 and 20% = 1642 cm−1) which further supports an interaction between Cdots and SSs.

As shown in Fig. 2(c), the HCSs have peaks at 1585 cm−1, 1065 cm−1, 796 cm−1 corresponding to aromatic rings, CC and CH vibrational modes, respectively. The typical peaks for CH stretching vibrations were observed at 2840/2910 cm−1 [46]. Upon incorporation of the Cdots on the surface of HCSs only the HCSs spectrum is observed. Further, the OH/NH and COO− region due to the Cdots does not grow in intensity with increase in % Cdots, suggesting that these groups could interact with the HCS surface. The peak positions for Cdots before and after incorporation on the surface of HCSs show insignificant shifts suggesting limited interaction of Cdots with the HCS surface (see Table S2).

The Cdots were also incorporated on the surface of the SCSs with different loadings (5% to 20%) (Fig. 2(d)). The functional groups detected for SCSs using FTIR were at 1195 cm−1, 1110 cm−1 , 1047 cm−1 and 887 cm−1+, corresponding to CO, CC, CH bending vibrations, respectively [47]. Upon incorporation of Cdots on the surface of SCSs, a slight increase in the peaks with increase in Cdots loading is observed at 1455 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, 1047 cm−1 and 887 cm−1 corresponding to CC, CO, C-C/C-O-C and CH bending vibrations. In summary the IR data indicate an interaction between the SSs and the Cdots, but the small peaks observed when the Cdots are supported on carbon (HCSs and SCSs) do not allow for conclusions to be drawn on interactions with these supports.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the XRD reflections of chitosan and the Cdots. Chitosan has two reflection peaks at 10.5 and 19.8° which are assigned to the amorphous and crystalline carbon framework, respectively [42]. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the Cdots have a broad reflection peak at 20.7° due to the presence of amorphous carbon which indicates that chitosan was fully carbonized [42]. The peak broadness is due to the highly disordered or amorphous carbon material, similar to the (200) planes of amorphous carbon [48,42,49].

Fig. 3(b) shows the XRD reflections of SSs and the Cdots/SSs composites. The broad reflection at 23.3° corresponds to the presence of amorphous SSs [41]. Upon incorporation of Cdots on the surface of the SSs the peak shifts slightly to lower 2θ values of 22.6° which is caused by the presence of the Cdots. The SSs spectrum overwhelms that of the Cdots so nothing more can be said about these composites.

Fig. 3(c) shows the reflections of the HCSs which has a broad amorphous carbon peak centred at 23° [50]. After incorporation of Cdots on the surface of HCSs the peak has broadened due to the presence of the Cdots.

The XRD reflections of SCSs and the Cdots/SCSs composites can be observed in Fig. 3(d). The SCSs have two reflections (25.2° and 43.9°) which were ascribed to the graphitic planes (002) and (001), respectively [47]. After the incorporation of the Cdots on the surface of SCSs a new shoulder-like peak is observed at 20.8° for all composites which grew with Cdots loading. The peak position did not change when compared to the unsupported Cdots indicating no change in the size of the carbon domains.

The thermal stability of the new materials is illustrated by the TGA and DTA thermograms in Fig. 4 . The thermal stability of Cdots was demonstrated by the TGA and DTA profiles which show two major weight loss steps (Fig. 4(a)). The gradual decomposition and loss of mass observed at 230 °C (39.5%) is due to the loss of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic, carbonyl, amines and carbon dioxide; these functional groups were identified in the FTIR spectra. The second weight loss at 620 °C is ascribed to the decomposition of the carbon part of the Cdots (surface and core carbons) [51].

The thermal stability of the SSs and the Cdots/SSs composites are shown in Fig. 4(b). A 5% weight loss for the SSs occurred up to 100 °C and is assigned to loss of moisture while the weight loss from 350 °C to 500 °C (~8%) is attributed to structural rearrangement and/or loss of hydroxyl groups [52]. The TGA curves for the Cdots/SSs composites are shown in Fig. 4(b). All composites show a weight loss of about 5% that occurred up to 100 °C due to moisture on the surfaces. Two other decomposition steps were observed between 150 °C to 240 °C and 350 to 500 °C that are due to loss of functional groups and carbon structures from the incorporated Cdots. The first decomposition step due to the loss of functional groups indicates a 4%, 6%, 8% and 15% weight loss for 5%Cdots/SSs, 10%Cdots/SSs, 15%Cdots/SSs and 20%Cdots/SSs composites, while the second decomposition peak caused by carbon structure decomposition has ~5% weight loss for all composites. In addition to TGA, DTA thermograms are plotted in Fig. 4(c). All composites showed two peaks between 110 °C and 220 °C (centred at 167 °C, 172 °C, 181 °C and 182 °C for 5%, 10%, 15% and 20% Cdots incorporation) and 240 °C to 400 °C (centred at ~330 °C) corresponding to decomposition of functional groups and carbon structure of the Cdots, respectively. In all composites, as expected, the intensity of both peaks increased as the amount of Cdots increased. Of note are (i) the lowered temperature of carbon decomposition and (ii) the change in intensity ratio of the carbon peaks on the silica. This suggests an interaction between the silica and the carbon.

Fig. 4(d) and (e) shows the TGA and DTA decomposition profiles of HCSs and Cdots/HCSs composites, respectively. The TGA curves for HCSs shows one major weight loss between 345 °C to 665 °C centred at 540 °C which is allocated to the complete decomposition of the carbon structure (Fig. 4(d)) [50]. After incorporation of Cdots on the surface of the HCSs to form Cdots/HCSs composites, two new peaks were observed between 120 °C to 240 °C and 260 °C to 380 °C which were ascribed to be due to loss of functional groups and the carbon structures associated with the Cdots. As seen in the DTA data (Fig. 4(e)), the thermograms for decomposition of functional groups from Cdots, the 5% loaded material has peaks centred at 198 °C and 305 °C while the peaks are centred at 188 °C and 330 °C for 10% to 20% loaded material. The peaks for the carbon decomposition from HCSs were shifted to lower temperature (510 °C) for the 5% Cdots loading and to higher temperature (HCSs = 540 °C) for the 10% to 20% material, it is clear that the thermal behaviour of the Cdots is influenced by the HCS support and that the decomposition of the carbon onto the HCSs after removal of the functional groups from the HCSs leads to a more stable carbon at higher loading (10% to 20%). This might be caused by kinks or rough surface present on HCS in which Cdots are trapped which results in an increased decomposition temperature [53].

The TGA and DTA decomposition profiles of SCSs and Cdots/SCSs composites are shown in Fig. 4(f) and (g), respectively. The TGA curve for SCSs alone shows one peak with the DTA thermogram peak centred at 675 °C resulting from the carbon structure decomposition [40]. Upon incorporation of Cdots on the surface of SCSs new peaks were observed at various decomposition temperatures. In all the Cdots/SCSs composites, some moisture loss (2% to 8%) loss is observed below 100 °C. Two peaks are also observed between 150 °C to 240 °C and 310 °C to 380 °C from the TGA curves which are ascribed to loss of surface functional groups, core functional groups and the carbon structure resulting from incorporated Cdots (Fig. 4(f)). The 5% loaded sample shows a shift in the peak at 180 °C relative to the other samples. As shown in Fig. 4(g), the DTA thermograms showed a significant peak shift toward lower temperature as the Cdots loading increased. The DTA peaks for SCSs is centred at 675 °C while for 5%Cdots/SCSs, 10%Cdots/SCSs, 15%Cdots/SCSs and 20%Cdots/SCSs composites are centred at 615 °C, 515 °C, 490 °C, 475 °C, respectively. The remarkable feature here is that the Cdots have reduced the carbon oxidation reaction.

The optical properties of the Cdots, Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs composites were studied using PL spectroscopy. Fig. 5 (a) and the insert shows the PL spectrum of Cdots probed at excitation wavelengths between 350 nm to 500 nm at 10 nm intervals [54]. The PL spectrum for all composites showed a typical red shift of the peak maximum as the excitation wavelength increased [55,56]. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the maximum emission peak of the Cdots was observed at 510 nm with FWHM (full width at half maximum) of 101 nm when the excitation wavelength of 440 nm was used. Upon excitation with wavelengths from 350 nm to 440 nm an increase in peak intensity, a redshift and an excitation-dependence of the Cdots is observed [21]. Thereafter, a decrease in peak intensity upon excitation with wavelengths above 450 nm to 500 nm is noted (insert). The excitation-dependent and redshift optical properties were similar to data reported in previous studies for bare Cdots [42,57]. Here the PL emissions data are attributed to optical selection at different defects states, non-radiative recombination of excitons at defects sites and functional groups present in the Cdots [56,58,59].

The PL spectra of the differently loaded Cdots (5%–20%) on the different supports are shown in Fig. 5. Upon incorporation of the Cdots on the surface of the SSs, HCSs and SCSs, the maximum PL emissions (and FWHM) were observed at 448 nm (106 nm), 422 (101 nm) nm and 343 nm (75 nm) when the excitation wavelengths of 380 nm, 340 nm and 300 nm were used for the Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs composites, respectively. Furthermore, when an excitation wavelength of 380 nm was used on Cdots, Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs composites the PL emissions were observed at 472 nm, 450 nm, 445 nm and 440 nm, respectively. When using an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the PL emissions were observed at 486 nm, 466 nm, 460 nm and 462 nm for Cdots, Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs, respectively. The interactions between Cdots and the support differ and have resulted in the different maximum PL emissions.

The real-time UV–vis of the Cdots, Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HSCs and Cdots/SCSs was observed using 365 nm lamp illumination. Fig. 6 represent the images of the materials under natural and UV lamp illumination. All materials were glowing under UV lamp illumination and a light blue emission was observed for bare Cdots and Cdots/SSs, while dark blue emission was observed for Cdots/HSCs and Cdots/SCSs.

The effect of the support on the PL emissions of the Cdots were further studied by observing the relative photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) using the PL excitation wavelength of 350 nm and absorbance data (Fig. 7 and Table S4). The PLQY of the Cdots and the composites were determined by using quinine sulphate (QS) as reference (see Table S5 for integrated PL emission intensities and absorbance data). The concentrations of the Cdots, Cdots/SSs composites, Cdots/HCSs composites, Cdots/SCSs composites and QS were similar to those used in a previous literature report [31]. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the PLQY of the bare Cdots was calculated to be 34% which is higher and comparable to earlier reported data [31,42]. The PLQY for the 5%Cdots/SSs, 10%Cdots/SSs, 15%Cdots/SSs and 20%Cdots/SSs composites was calculated to be 15.4%, 42%, 33.7% and 35.3%, respectively. The PLQY for 5%Cdots/HCSs, 10%Cdots/HCSs, 15%Cdots/HCSs and 20%Cdots/HCSs composites was 5.1%, 6%, 7.8% and 13%, respectively, while the PLQY for 5%Cdots/SCSs, 10%Cdots/SCSs, 15%Cdots/SCSs and 20%Cdots/SCSs composites was calculated to be 2%, 3.2%, 4.6% and 7.5%, respectively. The PLQY when Cdots are supported on HCSs and SCSs are lower than that supported on SSs. A decrease in PLQY was also observed in a previous study when polymer Cdots were supported/entrapped on a carbon support [33]. The decrease in PLQY as a result of decrease in PL emission can be attributed to a higher content of graphitic carbon on the support of the HCSs and SCSs. The core carbon of the HCSs and SCSs supports is likely to inhibit charge carrier recombination which greatly suppress the radiative recombination of the Cdots. This is supported by the CHNS composition data whereby an increase in carbon content resulted in decreasing PL intensity. The carbon content for Cdots/SSs, Cdots/HCSs and Cdots/SCSs was 8.26%, 52.14% and 55.18%, respectively. In addition, the higher PLQY and intense PL emission in bare Cdots is attributed to the higher content of nitrogen groups (6.95%) (see Table S3).

The differences in PL emissions when Cdots are supported on SSs, HSCs and SCSs is consistent with the synthesized Cdots having multistate (size, defects or functional groups and core carbon) that are responsible for the PL emissions [41,60]. In summary, the optical properties of Cdots are influenced by using different supports, as seen by the changes in the PL maximum emissions and PLQY of bare Cdots and Cdots supported onto SSs, HCSs and SCSs surfaces [24,61].

The N-doped Cdots used in this study had the same size distribution implying that the PL data changes would be only due to the support, surface-support interactions and support functional groups. Previous reports have demonstrated that the PL emissions of Cdots can also be changed by varying solvent pH, and changing the solvent or water content [23,57]. A decrease in PL emission (lowered PLQY) has often been observed. For example, Cdots supported on metal organic frameworks showed a decreased PLQY even though the Cdots maintained their shape and functional groups [23]. A study by Chowdhury et al., demonstrated that the addition of metals to Cdots (Cdots/Au and Cdots/Ag) enhanced the Cdots PL emissions via plasmonic effect [57]. In addition, the photoluminescence of Cdots was modified by varying the Cdots surface amino-groups density [14]. Thus the fluorescent properties of Cdots can be altered without changing the Cdots structure/shape and maintaining surface groups.

In the current study, TEM and XRD analysis, not unexpectedly, suggested little changes in the shape and size of the bare Cdots after being supported on both silica and carbon supports. The FTIR data are suggestive of interactions between the Cdots and the supports, especially as seen for the Cdots/SSs composites. TGA data showed remarkably different data for the Cdots on the three different supports. The Cdots-support interactions also led to significant changes in the photo-luminescent properties of the bare Cdots (maximum PL emissions and PLQY values) relative to the Cdots/support composites. The Cdots supported on SSs showed an enhanced PLQY as compared to carbon support which can be attributed to the Si-O(H) groups as suggested in previous studies. In addition, the silica supports have been reported to inhibit the self-quenching in Cdots [62–64].

The changes in maximum PL emissions and the lower PLQY when Cdots are supported on HCSs and SCSs can be related to the Cdots functional groups, different interactions between Cdots and support or the existence of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from Cdots to support [65]. PLQY of polymer Cdots supported on a carbon support was lowered with no clear explanation being given [33]. While another explanation drawn from previous study of NCQDs/TiO2 composites in which the lower PLQY was attributed to the injection of photogenerated electrons from Cdots to TiO2 [36]. It have been reported that the photoluminescence of Cdots can be quenched by electron acceptor or donor molecules in solution, with photoexcited Cdots being very good electron acceptor or donor [66]. In here, the lower PLQY when Cdots are supported on carbon supports (HCSs and SCSs) can be explained to be resulting from a possibility of photoinduced electron transfer from Cdots to carbon support (mainly caused by similar band gap or the zig-zag of the surface or core carbon framework). This implies that the surface sites of the carbon supports facilitate the trapping of the photoinduced electrons and holes from Cdots by acting as either electron acceptor or donor that lead to lower PL emission or reduced PLQY [66,67]. The PL emission intensity when Cdots are supported on carbon support is weaker due to the graphitic carbon of the supports which suppress the radiative recombination processes of the photoinduced electron/holes of the Cdots, as such the PLQY data indicates that the carbon support (HCSs and SCSs) are less effective than silica support at enhancing PLQY [68].

The major drawback that limits the application of Cdots in solid-state is their fluorescence self-quenching or aggregate-induced quenching [64]. This study has demonstrated a common and facile method for incorporation and good dispersion of the Cdots on the surface of SSs, HCSs and SCSs. The Cdots/support nanocomposites demonstrated fluorescence emission properties and can be recommended for application in the photonics, optoelectronics devices and photochemistry [69].

In summary, nitrogen doped carbon dots having a size below 10 nm have been synthesized by hydrothermal carbonization methods using chitosan as both carbon and nitrogen precursor. The study demonstrated a systematic method for the incorporation and distribution of Cdots on the surface of silica spheres, hollow carbon spheres and solid carbon spheres. The agglomeration of Cdots on the surface of supports was suppressed by the mixture of ammonia, ethanol and water, and the lower concentration of Cdots which facilitated the dispersion of the Cdots. The strong PL excitation-dependence of bare and supported carbon dots was observed when different PL excitation wavelengths were used. The incorporation of the Cdots on the surface of silica spheres, hollow carbon spheres and solid carbon spheres was observed to influence the maximum PL emissions. The silica support was found to induce larger PLQY as compared to the carbon supports.
